data.title: Grong Sparebank Q4 Pretax Profit Falls To NOK 35.5 Mln
data.description:
Grong Sparebank :

  • Q4 NET INTEREST INCOME NOK 62.6 MILLION VERSUS NOK 61.5 MILLION YEAR AGO

  • Q4 PRETAX PROFIT NOK 35.5 MILLION VERSUS NOK 70.0 MILLION YEAR AGO

data.title: Caledonia Mining Names Chester Goodburn as Next CFO
data.description:
Caledonia Mining Corporation said Wednesday it has appointed Ross Jerrard as its next chief financial officer, succeeding Chester Goodburn.

Goodburn will step down from the role on March 24. He will then assume a consultancy role to assist with the transition, the company said.

data.title: Makedonijaturist names new supervisory board chairman
data.description:
SKOPJE (N. Macedonia), February 19 (SeeNews) - North Macedonia's hotel and catering services company Makedonijaturist [MSE:MTUR] said on Wednesday it appointed Goran Markovski as new supervisory board chairman.

Markovski is replacing Done Tanevski, effective immediately, Makedonijaturist said in a bourse filing.

Tanevski served as supervisory board chairman since April 2023. Previously, he was the company's management board president.

Earlier this week, Makedonijaturist said it has replaced all members of its supervisory board.

The company's shares last traded at an average price of 10,850 denars ($184.3/176.5 euro) per share on the Macedonian Stock Exchange (MSE) on February 13.

(1 euro = 61.48 denars)

data.title: Newmark Group Names Stephen Merkel Chairman
data.description:
Newmark Group said late Tuesday it appointed Stephen Merkel board chairman following Howard Lutnick's resignation.

The company said Lutnick stepped down after being confirmed by the US Senate as the 41st secretary of commerce.

Merkel, who has been with Newmark since its acquisition by BGC Partners, will continue as executive vice president and chief legal officer, the company said.

data.title: Medley Logs 9% Higher Attributable Profit for 2024
data.description:
Medley recorded a profit attributable to owners of the parent of 2.80 billion yen for 2024, 9% higher than 2.57 billion yen logged for the preceding year.

The company's earnings per share for the reporting period increased to 85.62 yen from 78.55 yen per share a year ago, according to a Friday filing with the Tokyo Exchange.

Net sales of the medical human resources consulting company came in at 29.3 billion yen, 42.7% higher than 20.5 billion yen a year earlier.

It forecasted an attributable profit of 3.10 billion yen, a basic EPS of 95.33 yen, and net sales of 39.8 billion yen for the fiscal year 2025.

data.title: Zhejiang AngLiKang Pharmaceutical Gets Regulatory Acceptance for Cancer Drug Trial Application
data.description:
Zhejiang AngLiKang Pharmaceutical obtained acceptance from China's medical products administrator for its clinical trial application of ALK-N001.

ALK-N001 is a tumor microenvironment-activated small-molecule conjugate drug, according to a Wednesday filing with the Shenzhen bourse. Preclinical studies indicate ALK-N001 has significant tumor inhibition and good safety.

The pharmaceutical company holds development rights for the drug in mainland China, Hong Kong, Macau and Taiwan.

data.title: Sichuan Shudao Equipment & Technology Equipment Signs Intent Agreement for 65% Stake in Keyi Gas
data.description:
Sichuan Shudao Equipment & Technology signed an intent agreement to acquire a 65% stake in Henan Keyi Gas, according to a Tuesday filing with the Shenzhen Bourse.

The deal, involving cash payments, aims to enhance Shudao's industrial gas investment and operations.

While the agreement is non-binding, final terms will be determined after further negotiations and approvals.

data.title: BAE Systems Posts Double-Digit Jump in Sales as Defense Budgets Climb — Update
data.description:
By Cristina Gallardo

BAE Systems posted double-digit sales growth last year, boosted by increased defense budgets in its main markets due to Russia's war in Ukraine.

The London-listed aerospace and defense manufacturer said Wednesday that sales in 2024 rose by 14% on a constant-currency basis to 28.335 billion pounds, equivalent to $35.74 billion.

The FTSE 100 group, which makes a range of military equipment and provides cybersecurity services, said its order backlog reached a record 77.8 billion pounds at the end of 2024, beating a Visible Alpha estimate of 74.45 billion pounds. The company's order intake came to 33.7 billion pounds.

Orders were boosted by long-term programs in the U.K. and the U.S., warship contracts with Australia, increased munitions demand, and fresh orders for Eurofighter Typhoon fighter jets from Italy and Spain.

Underlying earnings before interest and taxes--the group's preferred measure of profitability--rose to 3.015 billion pounds from 2.68 billion pounds.

All business areas contributed to the results, with electronic systems and platforms and services units performing particularly well, BAE Systems said.

Earnings were further boosted by Colorado-based Ball Aerospace, a supplier of satellite systems, antenna arrays and other location products bought by BAE Systems in a bid to deepen its relationship with the National Aeronautics and Space Administration. Its integration into the group's U.S.-based Space and Mission Systems business is going well and enabling cost synergies at SMS, BAE Systems said.

"We remain confident in the positive momentum of our business into the future," Chief Executive Charles Woodburn said, citing the company's record order backlog.

The British company is waiting for clarity on the North Atlantic Treaty Organization's new defense-spending target, which allies are expected to set at the group's annual summit in The Hague in late June, Woodburn told investors during a call Wednesday. NATO's Secretary General Mark Rutte suggested last week that the target should rise from the current 2% of members' gross domestic product to "considerably" higher than 3% in order to fill holes in the alliance's military preparedness and to help deter Russia from any potential attacks in the future.

Woodburn said BAE Systems needs clarity on NATO's medium and long-term military requirements in order to plan production accordingly.

As European governments grapple with the need to consolidate the number of military platforms they use and increase weapons productions across the continent, Woodburn suggested that MBDA--a joint venture between Airbus, BAE Systems and Leonardo to produce missiles--could serve as a potential blueprint for more pan-European collaborations.

The group is also ramping up ammunition production, with new factories coming into operation to meet higher demand for artillery shells across Europe. It hopes in the coming months to increase ammunition production eightfold compared with levels before Russia's large-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, Woodburn said.

For 2025, BAE Systems guided for sales growth of up to 9% and organic growth of 6%-8%, while earnings should rise by between 8% and 10%, it said.

Matt Dorset, equity analyst at Quilter Cheviot, said the company has substantial exposure to European demand, just as the continent, including the U.K., prepares to raise defense spending. "We continue to see structural tailwinds supporting BAE, driven by increased global defense spending," he said.

BAE Systems' board declared a dividend of 20.6 pence, taking the total dividend to 33 pence a share, up 10% on year.

In morning trading, London-listed shares were down 2.1% at 13.08 pounds. The stock has risen 5.2% in the last 12 months.

Write to Cristina Gallardo at cristina.gallardo@wsj.com

data.title: China Electric Equipment Group Acquires Stake in Hsino Tower Group
data.description:
Hsino Tower Group was partly acquired by China Electric Equipment Group through a state-owned equity transfer, according to a Tuesday filing with the Shanghai Bourse.

The deal involves 843,257,367 shares, representing 31.52% of Hongsheng Huayuan's total shares. The acquisition complies with Chinese securities regulations and is exempt from a mandatory tender offer.

The transaction was finalized in Feb. 2025, with full disclosure of ownership stakes. China Electric Equipment Group has affirmed the authenticity and completeness of its acquisition report.

data.title: Baidu Is Maintained at Outperform by Mizuho
Howard Hughes Gets Revised Bid From Bill Ackman's Pershing Square
data.description:
Howard Hughes said late Tuesday its board received a revised, unsolicited proposal from Pershing Square Capital Management to acquire the company.

The company said a special committee will evaluate the revised proposal to "determine the appropriate course of action and process."

Bill Ackman is the founder and chief executive of Pershing Square Capital Management, which expressed interest in Howard Hughes in August followed by a formal takeover bid on Jan. 13.

data.title: Titan Cement International to Sell 75% Stake in Turkish Cement Company
data.description:
Titan Cement International signed a definitive agreement to sell its 75% stake in a company that owns cement assets in the eastern region of Türkiye for $87.5 million in cash proceeds.

The cement and building materials producer will sell 50% of its stake in Adocim Cimento Beton Sanayi ve Ticaret to Mugla Cimento Sanayi ve Ticaret and the remaining 25% holding to Yurt Cimento Sanayi ve Ticaret, according to a Wednesday release. The sale includes an integrated cement plant, a cement terminal, and all related infrastructure.

The deal is expected to close in the second quarter after it receives the necessary regulatory approvals. The company plans to maintain its cement grinding and supplementary operations in other regions of the country.

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0828 GMT - Glencore switching its listing to the U.S. would be a positive catalyst for its shares, Jefferies analysts Christopher LaFemina and Patricia Hove write. The Anglo-Swiss miner said Wednesday that it is reviewing its primary-listing status. Glencore's $1 billion share buyback also helps ease concerns over the slight miss to 2024 earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization expectations, they write. Meanwhile, Glencore is not expected to benefit from strong commodity tailwinds until next year, they write. Shares trade down 3% at 342.7 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0730 GMT - Comex gold futures' bullish momentum has reaccelerated, based on the daily chart, RHB Retail Research's Joseph Chai says in a commentary. Tuesday's price movements indicate the precious metal has resumed its bullish trajectory after undergoing profit-taking last Friday, the analyst notes. The relative strength index is rounding upward, signaling that bullish momentum is picking up speed, Chai says. The commodity is trading above both 20- and 50-day simple moving averages, which maintains the bullish technical setup. The nearest resistance is pegged at $3,000/oz, the analyst adds. Spot gold is little changed at $2,934.78/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0239 GMT - Iron ore prices are higher amid better sentiment. BHP, the world's largest listed miner, flagged tentative signs of recovery in the Chinese property sector, ANZ Research says in a commentary, noting that its CEO Mike Henry said that they are seeing "green shoots" in China's long-beaten property sector. Investor sentiment is boosted after Chinese President Xi Jinping's meeting with the country's tech leaders earlier this week, with Xi having urged them to maintain a "competitive spirit," signaling Beijing's support of the private sector, ANZ says. The most-traded iron ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is 1.2% higher at 818.00 yuan a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

1354 GMT - Antofagasta is expected to report a big jump in earnings in 2025 and 2026, AJ Bell investment director Russ Mould writes. The London-listed miner expects to dig out between 660,000 and 700,000 tons of copper in 2025, compared with 664,000 tons in 2024. Higher output and lower production costs should result in higher profits, he writes. However, the trajectory of copper prices over the rest of the year will have a significant impact, he adds. The miner is expected to report dividend growth in 2025 after three consecutive decreases from 2021's high of 142.5 U.S. cents per share, Mould writes. For 2024, the miner reported a full-year dividend of 31.4 U.S. cents. Shares trade up 0.5% at 1,845.50 pence.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1238 GMT - Global equities are expected to overtake their U.S. counterparts as the best-performing asset in 2025, Bank of America's February global fund manager survey finds. This month 34% of investors named global equities as the best-performing asset this year, up from 21% in January, the survey finds. Meanwhile, 18% of investors named U.S. equities as the best performing asset this year, down from 27% last month, the survey says. Gold is expected to be the second-best performing asset in 2025, according to 22% of surveyed investors. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

1010 GMT - Palm oil ended lower amid likely profit-taking after previous session's gains and concerns over weak export demand, according to Kenanga Futures says in a research note. However, the tightening palm oil stocks in Malaysia could provide support for the vegetable oil's prices, it adds. Meanwhile, Indonesia's new regulation requiring exporters of resources--excluding oil and gas--to keep all export proceeds locally for at least a year starting in March could pose challenges for Indonesian palm oil exporters, Phillip Nova analyst Lim Tai An says in a note. The move could benefit Malaysian exporters, who are poised to compete for more market share and support palm oil prices, he adds. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for May delivery was 34 ringgit lower at 4,508 ringgit a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0953 GMT - Antofagasta's lower-than-expected net debt comes at a good time given its capital expenditure is forecast to jump in 2025, Peel Hunt analysts write. The Chilean miner's net debt at the end of 2024 was $1.6 billion versus the $2.6 billion the analysts had expected. In 2025, capital expenditure is expected to hit $3.9 billion as it hits peak spend on its Centinela concentrator. This is Antofagasta's highest ever capex budget, the analysts write. Antofagasta reported a final dividend of 23.5 U.S. cents a share, which was higher than the 20 U.S. cents a share the analysts had expected. Shares trade up 3.2% at 1,894.50 pence. Over the past year, they are up 6.6%. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

data.title: BOE Will Question Transitory Nature of Inflation — Market Talk
data.description: While the Bank of England may want to view rising inflation as temporary, January data showing it rose to 3.0% suggest it could be a while before the base rate is cut again, says Jeremy Batstone-Carr, strategist at Raymond James Investment Services. The key question for the bank's monetary-policy committee is how transitory these price pressures will be, he says. The BOE voted unanimously in favor of cutting its interest rate two weeks ago, but since then its chief economist Huw Pill has cautioned against cutting rates in haste, despite weak economic activity. "Today's data vindicates this steadfast commitment to a gradual approach to further policy easing," Batstone-Carr says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0915 GMT - China's domestic retail sales of cars are expected to rise by 3% in 2025 compared to the previous year, according to Goldman Sachs analysts in a note. The country's passenger vehicle industry saw significantly stronger retail volumes in the 4Q 2024, thanks to expanded trade-in subsidies introduced at the end of July, they note. GS estimates that a total of 1.7 million vehicle sales were driven by the government subsidy last year. With government stimulus for autos extended into this year, GS raises its China passenger vehicle retail forecast for 2025 to 23.6 million from 22.1 million. Sales of new energy vehicles, including both electric vehicles and hybrids, are expected to capture an even larger market share this year, due to trade-in subsidies, strong model launches and price reductions, GS says. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0853 GMT - Retail sales volume of Chinese automobiles is expected to drop by 5% in 2025 compared to the previous year, according to Bernstein analysts in a note. They attribute this to demand being brought forward by China's revamped trade-in policy and local government subsidies. China faces a challenging macroeconomic outlook and weak consumer confidence, the analysts also note, adding that exports of Chinese cars, however, are expected to remain a growth driver. With relatively lean inventory levels, industry wholesale volumes may grow by 1%, reaching around 27.5 million units this year, Bernstein adds. The brokerage maintains a cautious outlook on China's auto sector and names BYD and Li Auto as their most preferred within the sector, with outperform ratings. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0843 GMT - Shares in easyJet and TUI fall, dragged by leisure-travel group peer Jet2's warning that its profit margin could come under pressure. easyJet shares in London trade down 2.9% at 502.60 pence while shares for German travel company TUI are 4% lower at 6.81 euros. Jet2 shares are down 9.65% at 14.14 pounds. Jet2 said that although bookings are up on-year for April, May and June, current macro-economic conditions, pressure on consumer incomes and customers' preference for booking late risk weighing on its performance. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

0737 GMT - Germany's DAX index could come under pressure today after President Trump spoke about new tariffs on cars, pharmaceuticals and semiconductors, IG says. "With new statements from Trump about tariffs, the DAX could possibly be the big loser of the day, as cars and pharmaceuticals are a large part of the index." However, it's unclear when these tariffs would be implemented and more information will come on April 2, IG says. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0712 GMT - Markets this morning will be processing President Trump's plan to impose 25% tariffs on imported cars, semiconductors and pharmaceuticals, analysts at LBBW Research say. Trump announced the tariffs Tuesday, adding that they could rise substantially over the course of the year and might come on top of reciprocal tariffs the administration says will equalize U.S. tariffs with the duties charged by other countries. "This would hit the export-oriented German automotive industry hard," LBBW Research adds. Trump plans to announce further details at the beginning of April. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0659 GMT - The car industry is back in focus after President Trump said he was considering tariffs of 25% on autos, semiconductors and pharmaceuticals. Analysts at Nomura noted that the U.S. is the largest export market outside of the eurozone for German car manufacturers, and Trump has taken particular issue with the European automotive sector and Europeans not buying U.S. cars. "Some car manufacturers already forewarned factory closures and layoffs prior to Trump's election in November 2024, and the prospect of U.S. tariffs only worsens the situation," they write. Nomura also expects the U.S. administration to apply reciprocal tariffs sometime in April. Currently, the U.S. has a 2.5% tariff on European cars, while the EU charges U.S. imports 10%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0411 GMT - J&T Global Express's Southeast Asian business might not grow as fast as the Chinese delivery company expected, Morningstar's Chelsey Tam says in a commentary. The analyst expects the company's Southeast Asia express volume share to increase to 29% by 2028. That compares with J&T Express's goal of quickly increasing its volume market share in Southeast Asia to 40%, she notes. Increasing gross merchandise volume share gains for e-commerce platform Shopee and express volume share gains for its logistics arm, SPX Express, will limit J&T's upside volume share gain potential, Tam says. She keeps a fair-value estimate of HK$6.10 on J&T Express. Share are last at HK$6.54. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0304 GMT - The financial capability of Airports of Thailand's key concessionaire is concerning, UOB Kay Hian write in a research report. They highlight a negative surprise regarding King Power's liquidity issue, which impacted AOT's 1Q FY 2025 earnings. The analysts are concerned about AOT, as King Power's financial position requires close monitoring. Additionally, the auction process for a new concessionaire is expected to take time, meaning AOT won't receive concession revenue in the interim, they add. Furthermore, the minimum guarantee payment from the new concessionaire is unlikely to match King Power's current fee of THB15 billion/year. As a result, the brokerage downgrades its rating to hold from buy, and lowers its target price to THB52.00 from THB70.00. Shares closed 0.6% higher at THB43.50 on Tuesday. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0058 GMT - Japanese auto stocks are lower after President Trump said he was considering tariffs of 25% or more on automobiles. The auto tariffs could negatively affect sales of Japanese cars or their profitability in the U.S. market. Toyota Motor, for instance, sold 2.3 million cars in the U.S. last year. Of that, about 23% were exported from Japan. The administration has said that action is likely after April 1. Trump said he would allow "a little bit of a chance" to re-shore production, without providing details. Toyota Motor is down 0.8%, Honda Motor is 0.9% lower and Subaru Corp. is down 1.9%. The benchmark Nikkei Stock Average is flat. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0038 GMT - ARB Corp.'s bull at Citi is feeling more confident in the Australian auto-accessory retailer's U.S. strategy. Analyst Sam Teeger acknowledges gross margins are under pressure from the weaker Aussie dollar but points to the emerging profitability of ARB's part-owned 4 Wheel Parts business. Teeger, who hadn't expected breakeven until fiscal 2027, says this shows strong execution and highlights a 25% increase in ARB product sales by 4WP since its acquisition. Citi lifts its target price 4.0% to A$51.20 and keeps a buy rating on the stock, which is down 0.3% at A$39.76. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1534 GMT - Southwest Airlines' decision to shed about 1,750 jobs is hard to square away like the others it's issued since its September investor day, says Melius Research's Conor Cunningham and Patrick Coleman in a research note. The analysts say the culture instilled at the carrier is a big factor in its successful history. "The news yesterday, to terminate 15% of corporate positions, goes against much of that culture the company has cultivated over its 53-year history," the analysts say. Other changes at Southwest included eliminating its open seating policy, which came in the wake of activist Elliott Investment Management's stake in the company. "A delicate balance is very much needed here, as culture is difficult to cultivate but easy to ruin," say the analysts. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1329 GMT - Amid talks on ending the war in Ukraine, Deutsche Bank has examined which truck manufacturer could benefit most from rebuilding the country, but says the country's truck market is small. The undisputed market leader is Volvo, followed by Traton, but given Volvo also has a construction equipment business, it could gain a slightly larger positive earnings impact. "Therefore, we would place the Swedish company in a 'Rebuild Ukraine basket'." Deutsche Bank believes that increased momentum in continental Europe, stabilization of the supply chain, and potentially lower raw material prices will likely outweigh the effect of a Ukrainian truck market recovery. Volvo shares rise 0.8% to 333.80 Swedish kronor. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Australian Leading Economic Index Strikes 30-Month High
data.description:
Pointing to a gradual recovery, an Australian leading economic index struck a 30-month high in January, reported the bank Westpac on Wednesday.

The Westpac-Melbourne Institute Leading Index, which indicates the likely pace of economic activity three to nine months into the future, lifted to 0.58% in January from 0.24% in December 2024, said Westpac.

Notably, all eight subcomponents of the Leading Index in January contributed positively to the six-month annualized growth rate, "the first time we have seen a consistent above-trend signal since June 2021," said Westpac.

Among the subcomponents, the commodity prices index largely stabilized in January, of importance to Australia's somewhat resource-dependent economy, noted Westpac.

Also improved in January were consumer unemployment expectations, dwelling approvals (residential building permits) and "a widening yield spread, a signal that broadly implies financial markets expect activity to pick up," said Westpac.

A rising domestic stock market "has also contributed positively," added Westpac.

The above-trend momentum on the leading economic index "is becoming more convincing," said Westpac.

However, the index still points to a "gradual recovery rather than a sudden surge," said the bank.

Westpac noted that the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) next meets on March 31-April 1. While the central bank cut rates at its recent policy session, "the case is unlikely to be convincing enough for the RBA Board to move (cut) again at its next meeting," advised Westpac.
